title: Talking about my life - Boulder, Colorado 15/21

transcript.text: We roamed around the cemetery and then around town and we thought we were all badass because of that or something.
It was exciting because we skipped school.
And then the next day at school, we didn't get busted because we thought we were going to get busted.
That was part of the excitement.
And another thing that we weren't allowed to do at that school was leave campus at lunch.
The only reason I could leave campus was because I had an off-campus pass because I lived one block, two blocks from school.
But I think they even revoked that.
And so kids basically weren't allowed to leave campus.
So if you left campus, like girls, for some reason, love to play the cop.
And this goes way back to the beginning of time, man.
Like if you turn around and you try to cheat off of a girl, they're always like, no.
Like girls are the perfect fucking society conformists.
They'll conform to the rules of society and enforce that shit.
Because like if you try to, if anybody tries to leave campus, you know some girl's going to be like, you, leaving campus.
I saw him leaving, trying to leave campus.
And so, yeah, you basically were going to get, probably were going to get busted because some girl was going to see you.
But, um, and kids were getting busted all the time and having to have like detention and stuff.
And I snuck off campus a couple times with some friends and we never got busted.
But it was definitely something you had to be really careful about.
Because the year before, some kid named, um, what was that kid's name?
He was one year older than me.
Um, McCarty.
Chance McCarty.
Um, got hit by a car and was in a coma for like three weeks and almost died.
And because, because he was off campus.
And so they revoked, they made it so kids couldn't leave campus that year after that.
And, um, so yeah, we had to hang out on the school campus.
So anyways, those are the only kids who I remember hanging out with seventh grade.
I didn't hang out with anybody.
Um, but like, I mean, they're, and then like in seventh and then like in eighth grade,
Halfway through eighth grade, we moved to like northern part of Boulder.
So I wasn't close to school anymore.
So I, so we ended up, I, I'd ride my bike to school, but sometimes I take the bus.
And I think maybe even most of the time I take the bus, which in retrospect sounds completely insane.
Like, why not just ride your bike?
I guess I just didn't have the energy because I remember I didn't have energy.
Eighth grade was just as bad as seventh grade.
Cause I remember, I remember walking to the bus stop after school every day and being like,
Because the teachers are maybe the students too, but the whole vibration,
The whole energy of school back then was, uh, was a vibration of, it was a reality,
A mindset of like, this is the only life you're ever going to live.
Like very limiting.
And so I'd be walking to the bus stop and like, you know, as soon as I leave the vortex of the school,
I'd be like, there must be something beyond this.
Cause I grew up being Buddhist, you know, there must be, I mean, I was even like forgetting there was such thing as reincarnation.
And I was getting so hypnotized by the reality of that place.
I'd be like, there must be other lives.
There must be angels.
It can't be all this.
This can't be the only reality we'll ever experience.
It's we have to be more than this.
It was just, it tripped me out.
It totally freaked me out.
And, um, yeah, but, um, I think I started hanging out with some more kids in eighth grade though.
So that's when I made friends with Mike Chapin.
He's, and he's like one of my best friends these days.
But, uh, I made friends with him cause he was in this, um, he was in theater class and that was a fun class.
Cause we, we did like, we partook in a, uh, a theater, theatrical production that I was the lead for.
I was this guy who dressed up like a girl trying to pretend like he was a girl for some reason.
Um, but, um, so I started being friends with him and then, so he kind of introduced me to some of the kids he was hanging out with.
Like he called it Ryan, Ronnie Earhart and those guys, not like Ronnie Earhart was like popular or anything,
But it was like Ronnie Earhart and like Kale Russell, Doug Jones, um, Mark, Mark, uh, Palmer.
And so I'd hang out, I'd kind of hang out with them for a little bit at lunch.
And then Mark Palmer's like, let's go take a hike.
So like I'd walk around campus with Mark Palmer.
So he kind of became my friend for a while in eighth grade.
And, um, but then I'd kind of, and then in ninth grade, I started hanging out with Dew again.
Dew started being my friend again.
He was, and he was hanging out with a kid named Dan Tonelli, who was in my choir class.
Um, who was actually, I think he was a year younger, a year below us.
I'm out with them.
And, uh, we didn't really hang out with Mike Chapin that much though.
Cause I remember we'd go in and be Mike Chapin, we'd be out there like eating his lunch out of his bagged lunch.
And we'd be like, Hey man, you want to come and hang out with us out there?
And he'd be like, what?
No, man, I'm reading this book.
And I remember Mike Chapin had this, like, he liked to repeat this thing a lot.
He was like, Lewis Carroll was on acid when he wrote Alice in Wonderland, man.
Lewis Carroll was, and you know what?
Jimi Hendrix, Jimi Hendrix wore those bandanas on his forehead cause he was on acid every time he played guitar, man.
I was like, Oh really?
Okay.
Um, yeah, nobody knows that, but it's true or something like that.
Yeah.
And there's this other kid who people made fun of named Doug Boyd.
He was like two years older than us cause he had a big head.
He had some kind of mint problem.
That was going to actually end his life when he hit 65 or something.
And he gave a presentation on our school about it.
But, uh, but, um, yeah, I remember in seventh grade before I even knew who any of these kids were.
Um, some girl, uh, maybe it was, I don't remember who it was, but she de-pantsed Shannon Jones.
So you, I remember I looked over and saw her underwear and she's like started screaming, like terrified and crying and stuff.
And then in the ninth grade, I saw these other two kids who were like best friends, Kale Russell and Jake Opdahl fighting.
They were fist fighting in the hallway, but they were fighting like girls.
It was really funny.
And then I saw them like hanging out with each other the next day.
So, yeah.
Um, but I wasn't on any of the sports.
I was on, um, I was on, um, track and field in seventh grade.
Cause that was before I got diagnosed with that knee problem.
And I was the second fastest kid in the track meets at our school.
The fastest kid at our school was, uh, Cedric Hustved.
And he won.
He was the fastest kid in the whole district.
I think we went as far as district championships and I got fourth.
He won and I got fourth.
And he raced the Red Zinger, but he didn't do that well.
He only raced in 85 and I kicked his ass, but he was like this like outgoing kid, like
Blonde dude and kind of independent minded, but he was only there in seventh grade, but
He didn't, he didn't seem athletic.
He didn't seem like he cared about athletes at all, but he did the mile and he fucking
Kicked ass.
He was like bigger than me.
I don't know if that had anything to do with it, but, um, but yeah, I didn't do track in
Eighth and ninth grade.
I think Mike Chapin did cause he'd talk about it, but I didn't do it cause I had the like
The knee problem.
But, uh, but I ran a five 40 mile.
I remember in seventh grade, I don't know what Cedric did it in, but I ran a five 40
Mile and Pete Vordenberg ran a four 40 mile cause I remember it was exactly one minute
Faster than me.
He was in ninth grade and he was in my ski team.
Um, he went to the Olympics twice.
I think he was state champion too in high school.
That's what Mike Chapin said, I think, but I wasn't on the team then, but that was the
Only school team I ever did.
We had our school teams were like basketball and football, but I never tried out for it
Cause I wasn't like social at all.
And I didn't even understand the game, the rules and I didn't, nor did I care.
I just like my philosophy with school was as soon as school got out, I was going home.
I didn't want to have anything to do with that place.
And I wasn't even going to do, I was going to do like absolute minimal homework too.
That was my policy all through school, do absolute minimal homework.
It was like a way to watch it out of my system.
Um, yeah.
So, I mean, I think if I had dropped out of school, I would end up being more studious
And I would have ended up reading more books cause I would have felt more responsibility
For it.
But, uh, especially when it came to math, science, I mean, I did all the, I did all the
Homework.
I turned it in on time and I did everything and I, and I did study for the test as much
As I could, but I didn't go overboard.
I just, I just, I just do it all until I, until I felt like I knew all of it, but I didn't,
I wouldn't go over it again.
You know, I wasn't like a perfectionist.
So like in the multiple choice science type of tests, I'd usually get like an 88, like
A B plus or an A.
But I did have, I remember I had, um, I had physics class, ninth grade physics class with
This dude named Mr. Walker, who is actually our chaperone for this time.
A bunch of us went down to, uh, see a Disney world where we paid extra for it, but we did
A tour of Disney world.
Um, but this guy, Mr. Walker, man, and he was a pretty good teacher too, man.
He was a good teacher, but he did something really fucking.
Weird.
I had a B plus in his class and I got an A in the final exam, right?
So then it become time at the very last day, they were going to tell you what your grade
Was final grade for the class.
And he gives me my grade and I got a C for the class.
And so I walk up to him, you know, and I'm patiently standing in line and I go up to him
And I go, yeah, I need to talk to you.
He goes, no, I'm not talking to you.
I'm busy.
Get away.
And I'm like, no, but seriously, I don't ever ask you for anything.
You know, this is, this is for real.
I got to talk to you about something.
He's like, he's like, get out of here.
I'm like, dude, no, I, I'm going to talk to you about something.
And he's like, he's like, what?
And I go, dude, I had a B, I had a B plus in the class.
And then I got an A in the final and I showed him my A because I still had the paper.
I go, see, look, A.
And then I showed him how I had a B plus.
Like, I think I even had proof.
I had like, look, this isn't, this is my pre final grade B plus.
And so here's my grade where the final class is a C.
And he looked, I remember him looking at it and having this like look of defeat.
And then, so then he changed my, my grade to like a B and he just gave it back to me
And didn't say anything.
So I think he did that on purpose.
I think he, he needed to, to give like C's to a certain amount of kids to like keep up
The quota.
And I mentioned this to like a college, an ex college professor a couple of years ago
About that kind of stuff.
And he said he didn't know anything about that.
But so maybe kid teachers just don't like kids for some reason, but he did that.
And that another thing happened to me once too.
I was in like, um, choir, Mrs. Doran or Mrs. Rollins.
And I showed up every day.
I did this song perfectly.
I did everything and all the kids in my class, all the boys got A's, but I got to be, I was
The only kid who got to be, but I, and I, and the weird thing of what it was is a lot
Of kids like interrupted her a lot and would say stuff that had nothing to do with school
And like, and she'd have to like discipline them or whatever.
And I was never one of the kids who had to do that.
So yeah, I'm just something about my vibe.
I think I'm like spaced out.
Like I don't pay attention a lot.
I'm always like this and teachers see me doing that and they like pisses them off.
It makes them feel like they're not doing a good job or something.
So they want to like get revenge on me by giving me a bad grade.
Cause they, I mean, they did that in college too.
I had a college class and I never, I didn't call these ladies on it, but they, they gave
Me my, my grade and stuff and they go, yeah, here's your grade.
We don't know what it is yet, but it's, it's, it's going to be a B there's an, it's not going
To be lower than a B.
So I'm like, all right.
So then I get my thing in the, my grade in the paper and the mail and it's a C.
So they decided to change their grade also.
And also in education department, I got a D minus in the final paper and he didn't tell
Me I got a D minus until two years afterwards.
But because of that, and the reason I got a D minus was because I, uh, he said like
One of my chapters was the wrong title.
It should have been a title of another chapter.
Um, but he, this guy wasn't even a writer.
He was like a computer programmer who had a PhD.
So he got a job in the education department.
But because of that, they kicked me out of the hole in the education department and didn't
Let me do my student teaching and get my teacher license because of that.
So yeah, teachers, man.
Um, I can't, I'm not ever going to take a class again because teachers, they just, they
Just, they're out to get me, dude.
It's real.
It's not paranoia.
It's fucking real.
